critique.summary: The article briefly introduces Borderhold, a browser-based grand strategy game designed for hundreds of players on large maps. It provides a link to the game but contains no detailed information about gameplay, development, or player experience.
mainPoints: Borderhold is a browser strategy game.
It supports hundreds of players simultaneously.
The game features large maps for grand strategy gameplay.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Borderhold being a browser-based grand strategy game for many players on large maps are consistent with the game's official website and descriptions found online. However, the article lacks detailed information, sources, or perspectives to fully verify the scope or quality of the game.
considerations: The article does not provide information on the game's developer or publisher.
No details are given about gameplay mechanics, player reviews, or community size.
There is no mention of system requirements, monetization, or platform compatibility.
The article lacks multiple sources or perspectives to assess the game's reception or reliability.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on a browser-based grand strategy game supporting hundreds of players, with comments highlighting technical achievements, performance issues, and user experience concerns. Some users praise the underlying technology and scalability, while others criticize bugs, lack of gameplay depth, and poor UI feedback.

comments.author: sgolem
comments.text:
One of the main challenges has been making the map simulation work for hundreds of simultaneous players without the server becoming a bottleneck.

Most interactions are event-driven and the map state updates incrementally, which keeps things manageable even when many borders are shifting at once.

I've successfully tested this with 4096^2 maps and 1024 players and in those tests framerates were stable at 144 FPS, with the server ticking steadily at the standard 10 TPS.

It is built on Rust and Bevy. I'm happy to answer any questions.
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed technical insight into the game's architecture and performance testing, making it the most accurate and informative contribution.

comments.author: bschwindHN
comments.text:
The sliders don't respond well, like the server is authoritative but the client isn't properly predicting where the slider will be.

I'm playing on a 144Hz monitor, but the game is struggling to hit over 80 FPS.

I keep getting errors which display in the bottom right of the screen, such as "tick" (tick failed, ZeroInvestment) errors and WebSocket receive errors.

I started a custom game and "things" are just happening super quickly. All I can seemingly do is right click some stuff and do some actions.

There is almost no feedback on the few actions you can do. Nothing much to observe. I don't even know where "I" am on the map.

Sorry but this isn't a game yet. There's no compelling core gameplay loop, too little explanation, and wayyyy too many bugs.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a detailed user experience report with specific performance and UI issues, reflecting a realistic and critical perspective on the current state of the game.

comments.author: altairprime
comments.text:
> Curious what people think.

About what? The game itself is mildly interesting as some sort of blockchain mega-Risk variant, but without any sort of story about how you came up with the idea, or how you coded it, or a link to your source code, or etc etc etc this seems primarily meaningless/irrelevant here at Hacker News. I see your comment asking us to propose questions that you answer — but where are your own stories, that aren't just replies to the prompts you want us to present you with?

> You agree not to[:] reverse engineer or attempt to extract protected service logic except where the law clearly permits it

Talk about failing to understand your target audience.
comments.analysis: This comment critiques the lack of background information and transparency from the developer, which is a reasonable request in a developer-focused community like Hacker News.

comments.author: rcakebread
comments.text: Very buggy, so many it's not even worth reporting, since you can't be bothered to test.
comments.analysis: While this comment highlights bugs, it lacks constructive detail and is dismissive, reducing its usefulness.

comments.author: plz_throw
comments.text:
I don't know, looks like OpenFront in a RetroGUI? Based on the left bottom UI element an older version, otherwise remarkably close

[1] https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=ckqSFbwplvY
comments.analysis: This comment attempts to compare the game's UI to another known interface, providing a plausible observation but with limited impact on the overall discussion.

comments.author: brudgers
comments.text:
I clicked "Play Game" and got a message about a queue.

Maybe that experience can be improved.
comments.analysis: A brief but constructive comment suggesting improvement in user experience related to queuing.

comments.author: SilentM68
comments.text: Hmm, it does not seem to work on Edge browser on my Linux OS, but it loaded fine in Chrome in the same OS.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a useful compatibility note, indicating a potential browser-specific issue.

comments.author: beeflet
comments.text: I don't like the mixel art used
comments.analysis: This is a subjective aesthetic opinion with little substantive value to the technical or gameplay discussion.
